elements.sent-2411-001: Test collections are an integral part of Information Retrieval (IR) research.
elements.sent-2411-002: They allow researchers to evaluate and compare ranking algorithms in a quick, easy and reproducible way.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, constructing these datasets requires great efforts in manual labelling and logistics, and having only few human relevance judgements can introduce biases in the comparison.
elements.sent-2411-004: Recent research has explored the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) for labelling the relevance of documents for building new retrieval test collections.
elements.sent-2411-005: Their strong text-understanding capabilities and low cost compared to human-made judgements makes them an appealing tool for gathering relevance judgements.
elements.sent-2411-006: Results suggest that LLM-generated labels are promising for IR evaluation in terms of ranking correlation, but nothing is said about the implications in terms of statistical significance.
elements.sent-2411-007: In this work, we look at how LLM-generated judgements preserve the same pairwise significance evaluation as human judgements.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our results show that LLM judgements detect most of the significant differences while maintaining acceptable numbers of false positives.
elements.sent-2411-009: However, we also show that some systems are treated differently under LLM-generated labels, suggesting that evaluation with LLM judgements might not be entirely fair.
elements.sent-2411-010: Our work represents a step forward in the evaluation of statistical testing results provided by LLM judgements.
elements.sent-2411-011: We hope that this will serve as a basis for other researchers to develop reliable models for automatic relevance assessments.
elements.sent-2411-012: Information Retrieval (IR) research follows the Cranfield paradigm~\cite{Cleverdon1962 to evaluate retrieval systems in offline settings~\cite{Voorhees2001a .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Under this paradigm, test collections form the basis to compare the effectiveness of several systems~\cite{Sanderson2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Test collections comprise a set of topics and their relevance judgements (also called labels, assessments or qrels throughout this paper) against a given document corpus.
elements.sent-2411-015: In the early days of IR, collections were small enough that allowed for completeness: every document got its judgement with respect to every topic~\cite{Harman2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, these collections did not represent what commercial systems faced in the real world.
elements.sent-2411-017: There was a need to build larger collections.
elements.sent-2411-018: The problem is that relevance judgements are made by human experts, making it a very labour-intensive task.
elements.sent-2411-019: While this allows a great deal of assessments to be made, it is certainly impossible to manually review every document in modern test collections due to their size.
elements.sent-2411-020: Recently, several works explored the possibility of using LLMs to assess the relevance of documents~\cite{Upadhyay2024a,Upadhyay2024b,Abbasiantaeb2024,Rahmani2024a,Thomas2024,MacAvaney2023,Faggioli2023 , since they are considerable cheaper than humans and show strong text-understanding capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, LLMs could help to reduce the logistical problems of human judgements.
elements.sent-2411-022: In one way or another, all these works use ranking correlations, such as Kendall's $\tau$~\cite{Kendall1948 or rank-biased overlap (RBO)~\cite{Webber2010 , to validate the reliability of judgements generated with an LLM for IR evaluation.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The rationale is that if judgements generated by an LLM are able to provide a similar ranking of systems to the ranking inferred from human judgements, the LLM could replace the human in terms of capturing run-level effectiveness~\cite{Upadhyay2024b .}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, some recent works have demonstrated that rank correlation is not enough~\cite{Otero2023b,Kutlu2018b .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Although judgements generated by an LLM may give good results in terms of system rankings, we know nothing about whether the results of a statistical test between any two systems would yield similar results.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we aim to contribute to fill this gap.
elements.sent-2411-027: We evaluate if significance decisions regarding the differences between evaluated systems under LLM judgements are similar to those observed under human judgements.
elements.sent-2411-028: To this aim, we perform a multiple comparisons test between every pair of runs, both with human judgements and with LLM judgements.
elements.sent-2411-029: We propose several experiments and measures to evaluate how both tests' outputs agree.
elements.sent-2411-030: These experiments allow us to explore if LLM qrels can preserve significance decisions and to characterize where they fail.
elements.sent-2411-031: With this setup, we explored the following research questions:.
elements.sent-2411-032: Are the LLM judgements able to preserve the same statistically significant differences as the human judgements? Regarding this aspect, we found that they are, in fact, able to detect most of the ground truth differences while maintaining acceptable numbers of false positives.
elements.sent-2411-033: How close, in terms of effectiveness, are the pairs where LLMs qrels fail to detect significance? Regarding this question, we found that there are some cases where the LLM qrels fail to detect statistical significances that should be easy to detect.
elements.sent-2411-034: Among the evaluated systems, are there some that may not be being assessed fairly? Regarding this aspect, results suggest that some systems might be treated differently, since they concentrate a high rate of false negatives.
elements.sent-2411-035: Overall, our experiments suggest that LLMs are capable tools for automatic relevance judgements in IR evaluation but there is still room for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-036: This work constitutes a step forward in evaluating the statistical testing results derived from LLM qrels, which we hope will serve as a basis to other researchers to further assess the validity of using LLMs for automatic relevance assessments.
elements.sent-2411-037: We study the reliability of LLM-generated judgements to evaluate retrieval systems.
elements.sent-2411-038: Assuming that human judgements are the ground truth, we evaluate if the LLM labels are able to provide a similar pairwise significance evaluation, i.e. if decisions regarding the statistical difference between a pair of systems are the same using human labels and LLM labels.
elements.sent-2411-039: In this sectionm, we first give the details of our method and then propose a series of novel measures to evaluate the preservation of statistical pairwise decisions.
elements.sent-2411-040: Let $Q_{H}$ be the judgements made by human assessors over a set $T_{H}$ of topics, and let $Q_{LLM}$ be the judgements made by an LLM over a set $T_{LLM}$ of topics.
elements.sent-2411-041: We do not assume any overlap between $T_{H}$ and $T_{LLM}$, in fact, they may be completely disjoint sets and have different sizes.
elements.sent-2411-042: Let $R$ be the set of runs under evaluation, where each run was executed against all topics $T_{H \cup T_{LLM}$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Using an evaluation measure of choice (e.g. Average Precision), we compute per-topic scores for each run in $R$ using both set of judgements independently.
elements.sent-2411-044: Thus, for each run, we have two arrays of per-topic scores, one obtained using $Q_{H}$ and other obtained with $Q_{LLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-045: With this per-topic scores, we then compute a $p$-value for each pair of runs, for both set of qrels.
elements.sent-2411-046: So, at the end, we have, on the one hand, a $p$-value for each pair of runs computed from the scores obtained with $Q_{H}$ and, on the other hand, a different $p$-value for each pair of runs computed from the scores obtained with $Q_{LLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-047: Thus, for each pair of runs, given a significance level $\alpha$, we know if they are significantly different (or not) under $Q_{H}$ and under $Q_{LLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Now, assuming that the significance decisions derived from the $Q_{H}$ qrels are the ground truth, we evaluate how the decisions derived from $Q_{LLM}$ match the former.
elements.sent-2411-049: In particular, we compute the true/false positives/negatives:.
elements.sent-2411-050: True Positives (TP): these are the cases where both set of judgements marked a significant result.
elements.sent-2411-051: False Negatives (FN): these are the cases where a pair of runs is marked as significantly different under $Q_{H}$, but not under $Q_{LLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-052: True Negatives (TN): these are the cases where both set of judgements marked a non-significant result.
elements.sent-2411-053: False Positives (FP): these are the cases where a pair of runs is marked as non-significant under $Q_{H}$, but marked as significant under $Q_{LLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-054: We report these metrics as percentages over the gold counts of significant and non-significant.
elements.sent-2411-055: TP will be the percentage of correctly detected positives over the total of gold positives, FN will be the ratio of incorrectly detected negatives over the total of positives, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-057: We used the data of the SynDL dataset~\cite{Rahmani2024b to perform our experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-058: To the best of our knowledge, it is the only tests collection that, at the time of writing, includes relevance judgements both made by humans and by an LLM.
elements.sent-2411-059: This dataset is based on the passage ranking task of the Deep Learning Track, from years 2019 to 2023.
elements.sent-2411-060: Each year, organizers of the track created a set of queries from which some received human judgements.
elements.sent-2411-061: For example, in DL19, only 43 out of the 200 queries received expert assessments.
elements.sent-2411-062: The creators of SynDL used GPT-4 to provide labels for the rest of the queries that did not receive human judgements.
elements.sent-2411-063: We refer the reader to the paper for more details~\cite{Rahmani2024b .}
elements.sent-2411-064: It is important to note that, for every year, the set of topics that have human judgements and the set of topics that have LLM judgements are disjoint.
elements.sent-2411-065: We used both Average Precision (AP) and NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain) with a 1000 cutoff for scoring the runs.
elements.sent-2411-066: To compute the $p$-values, we employed the two-sided randomised TukeyHSD~\cite{Carterette2012,Sakai2018 with \num{100000 permutations, with the null hypothesis $H_0$ being the equality of mean scores, as usual in IR research, and set $\alpha = 0.05$.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \caption{Statistics of the datasets used for experimentation. Note that $T_{H} \cap T_{LLM} = \emptyset$.} \label{tab:data} \setlength\tabcolsep{2pt} \ra{1.2} \begin{tabular}{@{}lcrrrrr@{}} Data DL-19 DL-20 DL-21 DL-22 DL-23 Topics with human judgements ($\lvert T_{H} \rvert$) 43 54 53 76 82 Topics with LLM judgements ($\lvert T_{LLM} \rvert$) 157 146 424 424 618 \arrayrulecolor{gray!30} \arrayrulecolor{black} Average judgements per topic in $T_{H}$ 215 211 204 5084 272 Average judgements per topic in $T_{LLM}$ 70 128 157 133 73 \arrayrulecolor{gray!30} \arrayrulecolor{black} Submissions 36 59 63 100 35 Pairs of submissions 630 1711 1953 4950 595 \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-002: \caption{Classification results observed using the synthetic qrels on different datasets with AP and NDCG.} \label{tab:agreements} \setlength\tabcolsep{2pt} \ra{1.2} \begin{tabular}{@{}lllrrrrr@{}} \multirow{2.3}{*}{Metric} \multicolumn{5}{c}{Dataset} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2019} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2020} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2021} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2022} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2023} \multirow{4.3}{*}{AP} TP 95\% 99\% 100\% 98\% 98\% FN 5\% 1\% 0\% 2\% 2\% \arrayrulecolor{gray!30} \arrayrulecolor{black} TN 69\% 75\% 39\% 49\% 36\% FP 31\% 25\% 61\% 51\% 64\% \multirow{4.3}{*}{NDCG} TP 99\% 100\% 100\% 100\% 99\% FN 1\% 0\% 0\% 0\% 1\% \arrayrulecolor{gray!30} \arrayrulecolor{black} TN 37\% 73\% 43\% 52\% 37\% FP 63\% 27\% 57\% 48\% 63\% \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-067: We show the results of our experiments in \Cref{tab:agreements .}
elements.sent-2411-068: This table reports the TP/TN/FP/FN percentages both when scoring the runs with AP (upper half) and NDCG (lower half).
elements.sent-2411-069: We observe that LLM-generated labels detect most of the existing significant differences, since TP ratios are near 100\% or even reach the maximum in some cases.
elements.sent-2411-070: This result holds independently of using AP or NDCG, although TP figures obtained with NDCG are systematically higher.
elements.sent-2411-071: Interestingly, we observe a high number of false positives for every year and metric.
elements.sent-2411-072: These cases represent situations were the LLM judgements $Q_{LLM}$ marked a significant difference when there was none.
elements.sent-2411-073: In other words, some pairs of systems are not significantly different under $Q_{H}$ but under $Q_{LLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Without further analysis, this seems a concerning result, since it means that judgements created with an LLM would lead a researcher to incorrectly conclude and present a supposedly significant result.
elements.sent-2411-075: However, we hypothesize that this might be happening because of the difference in sample sizes between both evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-076: As observed in \Cref{tab:data , the number of topics that include LLM-generated labels is much higher than the topics that have human judgements.}
elements.sent-2411-077: It is known that more topics provide more power when performing a statistical test~\cite{Carterette2008,Sanderson2005,Webber2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Therefore, these false positives could be happening for this reason.
elements.sent-2411-079: To check this hypothesis, we sampled random subsets of $T_{LLM}$ so that $\lvert T_{H \rvert = \lvert T_{LLM \rvert$.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: In this way, both evaluations have an equal sample size.
elements.sent-2411-081: We performed this sampling 50 times, and computed the average of ratios over these 50 iterations.
elements.figure-2411-003: \caption{Classification results observed using the SynDL synthetic qrels on different datasets with AP and NDCG. We randomly undersample $T_{LLM}$ topics such that $\lvert T_{LLM} \rvert = \lvert T_{H} \rvert$.} \label{tab:agreements-sampling} \setlength\tabcolsep{2pt} \ra{1.2} \begin{tabular}{@{}lllrrrrr@{}} \multirow{2.3}{*}{Metric} \multicolumn{5}{c}{Dataset} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2019} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2020} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2021} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2022} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2023} \multirow{4.3}{*}{AP} TP 76\% 83\% 89\% 88\% 82\% FN 24\% 15\% 11\% 12\% 18\% \arrayrulecolor{gray!30} \arrayrulecolor{black} TN 97\% 96\% 88\% 81\% 73\% FP 3\% 4\% 12\% 19\% 27\% \multirow{4.3}{*}{NDCG} TP 85\% 95\% 93\% 90\% 92\% FN 15\% 5\% 7\% 10\% 8\% \arrayrulecolor{gray!30} \arrayrulecolor{black} TN 96\% 95\% 91\% 87\% 85\% FP 4\% 5\% 9\% 13\% 15\% \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-083: These results show that, indeed, under equal sample size conditions, the number of false positives is lower, supporting our previous hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-084: However, we also observe lower values of true positives.
elements.sent-2411-085: That is, now some differences that are real under $Q_{H}$ are being ignored under $Q_{LLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-086: Although one could argue that this is better than before, since a false negative can be interpreted as less harmful than a false positive, it is still concerning.
elements.sent-2411-087: Leaving some real advancements behind can also hinder progress in the field.
elements.sent-2411-088: Regarding the differences between true negatives and true positives, we see that LLM-generated judgements are equally effective at detecting true significances and non-significances.
elements.sent-2411-089: In this case, we also observe better figures with NDCG, a result inline with previous research in related fields~\cite{Valcarce2018c .}
elements.sent-2411-090: Overall, results of this experiment showcase that, in terms of preserving the statistical significances between systems, LLM qrels have an acceptable performance, although there is still room for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-091: In light of these results, we believe that one interesting aspect to evaluate is whether misses are over pairs that have a ``strong'' significant difference in the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-092: Given a significance level $\alpha$, e.g. 0.05, we argue that failures committed under LLM judgements would be less bad if they occur in pairs who have lower chances of being different, i.e. their $p$-value is closer to $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-093: In other words, it is not the same to miss a significant result that has a gold (i.e. under $Q_{H}$) $p$-value of $0.049$ than to miss a significant result that has a $p$-value of $0.001$.
elements.sent-2411-094: The lower the $p$-value, the lower the chances that the difference is due to the randomness samples observed in the experiment, thus it should be ``easier'' to detect.
elements.sent-2411-095: To evaluate this, we compute the Kendall's~$\tau$ correlation between the rankings of pairs inferred from the $p$-values.
elements.sent-2411-096: In particular, we rank each pair of runs by their given $p$-value in ascending order, so that the pairs that have a bigger difference---a lower $p$-value---are ranked higher.
elements.sent-2411-097: We obtain two different rankings of the same set of pairs, one under $Q_{H}$ and other under $Q_{LLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-098: The correlation is computed between these two rankings.
elements.sent-2411-099: The intuition is that a higher correlation suggests that wrong decision are not far away from being correct in terms of $p$-values differences, while a lower correlation would suggest that the evaluation with LLM judgements is missing differences that should be easy to detect.
elements.sent-2411-100: To emphasize the results on the most different pairs, i.e. those a top positions of these rankings, we also compute the top-weighted RBO ($p = 0.07$) correlation~\cite{Webber2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-102: As in the previous experiment, results are averages over 50 iterations of undersampling $T_{LLM}$, to maintain a comparable evaluation.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Correlations of rankings of run pairs, inferred from the $p$-values for each pair. Results are averages over 50 iterations of undersampling $T_{LLM}$.} \label{tab:kendall} \setlength\tabcolsep{2pt} \ra{1.2} \begin{tabular}{@{}lllrrrrr@{}} \multirow{2.3}{*}{Metric} \multicolumn{5}{c}{Dataset} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2019} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2020} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2021} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2022} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2023} \multirow{2}{*}{AP} Kendall's $\tau$ 0.97 0.98 0.96 0.98 0.95 RBO ($p = 0.07$) 0.43 0.82 0.83 0.98 0.53 \multirow{2}{*}{NDCG} Kendall's $\tau$ 0.97 0.97 0.98 0.97 0.97 RBO ($p = 0.07$) 0.43 0.96 0.82 0.99 0.53 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-103: We observe strong correlations\footnote{As a rule of thumb, we consider strong correlations those over 0.80.}
elements.sent-2411-104: Some concerning results are RBO values for DL-2019 and DL-2023.
elements.sent-2411-105: These values suggest that, in some cases, LLM qrels do not yield a reliable statistical pairwise comparison, since some pairs that are quite distant---the $p$-value under $Q_{H}$ is one of the lowest ones---are being marked as statistically equal.
elements.sent-2411-106: We lucubrate this might be due to the differences in the number of judgements per topic (see \Cref{tab:data ), although there is no evidence that supports this.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Correlations are useful to see in a global way whether the judgements produced by an LLM can maintain the pairwise statistical differences.
elements.sent-2411-108: However, we are also interested in whether any particular system has been treated differently, something we do not see with correlations.
elements.sent-2411-109: This is similar to what has been called fairness in past work~\cite{Voorhees2018,Otero2023a , where they evaluate the change that each run suffers in terms of positions in the ranking of systems.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Inspired by this idea, we perform a similar experiment to evaluate how the significances of a run change under LLM qrels.
elements.sent-2411-111: We are interested in knowing if a run has more or less significant differences.
elements.sent-2411-112: For every run, we count the number of significant differences against this run observed under the human judgements and under the LLM judgements.
elements.sent-2411-113: Then, a drop happens when the number is higher in the first case, meaning that a run is losing some significances under the LLM labels.
elements.sent-2411-114: In other words, we are counting the number of false negatives for each run.
elements.sent-2411-115: We also undersampled $T_{LLM}$ 50 times and averaged the results.
elements.sent-2411-116: We plot the distribution of these drops in \Cref{fig:fairness .}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fairness} \caption{Distribution of the drops in significance counts of the runs when evaluated with LLM labels. Each value is the average over 50 iterations of undersampling $T_{LLM}$.} \label{fig:fairness}
elements.sent-2411-117: This figure shows the distribution of the per-run drops, averaged over 50 undersampling iterations, for the different year and effectiveness measures we have used.
elements.sent-2411-118: The ideal case here would be to observe very narrow boxes located near 0, without outliers.
elements.sent-2411-119: This would mean that, on average, no run is treated differently in terms of significances.
elements.sent-2411-120: However, we observe that, to a greater or lesser degree, there is always some run than is treated differently, regardless of the year and the effectiveness measure.
elements.sent-2411-121: This suggests that the numbers of false negatives we observed in a previous experiment (see \Cref{tab:agreements-sampling ) are concentrated within just a few runs.}
elements.sent-2411-122: In other words, the false negatives appear because the LLM qrels are consistently failing to correctly evaluate the same few runs.
elements.sent-2411-123: For this reason, we conclude that LLM qrels are not fair for evaluating IR systems.
elements.sent-2411-124: Making relevance judgements is the most resource-intensive task when creating new collections for IR evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-125: Recent research has explored the possibility of using LLMs to assess the relevance of documents, with recent attempts showing promising results: LLMs can produce accurate labels compared to human assessments and reliable rankings of systems in terms of correlation.
elements.sent-2411-126: However, little work has evaluated the validity of these judgements in terms of statistical significance stability.
elements.sent-2411-127: The significant differences detected with human judgements and with LLM judgements might be completely different.
elements.sent-2411-128: In this work, we had a look at this issue and evaluated the reliability of LLM labels for IR evaluation from different perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-129: First, we evaluated if significance decisions regarding the differences between evaluated systems are similar to those observed under human judgements.
elements.sent-2411-130: Our results showed that, indeed, LLM-generated judgements detect most of the significant differences, although recall values are not perfect, suggesting that with this kind of judgements some advancements might be left behind.
elements.sent-2411-131: Second, we also looked at correlations between the rankings of pairs of runs in terms of $p$-values, showing that LLM-generated labels fail in some cases to detect differences that should be easy to detect.
elements.sent-2411-132: We also had a look at whether any of the runs, not just the best ones, suffer significant changes in their significance when evaluated with LLM judgments.
elements.sent-2411-133: In this experiment we observed that, indeed, there are runs that suffer drops of up to 50 positions, which suggests that these LLM-generated judgements are not entirely fair for evaluating IR systems.
elements.sent-2411-134: This is a step forward in validating the use of LLMs for relevance assessment.
elements.sent-2411-135: Overall, these findings suggest that LLMs offer valuable potential as tools to assist in creating test collections for IR, particularly by providing scalable and efficient options for generating relevance judgments for large document corpora.
elements.sent-2411-136: In contexts where human resources are limited, LLMs may augment human judgment in meaningful ways, and in fact, efforts in this line are starting to appear~\cite{Takehi2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-137: However, as prior studies have noted~\cite{Soboroff2024, Faggioli2023 , LLMs are not without their limitations.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Offline evaluation of search systems depends on test collections.
elements.sent-2411-139: These benchmarks provide the researchers with a corpus of documents, topics and relevance judgements indicating which documents are relevant for each topic.
elements.sent-2411-140: While test collections are an integral part of Information Retrieval (IR) research, their creation involves significant efforts in manual annotation.
elements.sent-2411-141: Large language models (LLMs) are gaining much attention as tools for automatic relevance assessment.
elements.sent-2411-142: Recent research has shown that LLM-based assessments yield high systems ranking correlation with human-made judgements.
elements.sent-2411-143: These correlations are helpful in large-scale experiments but less informative if we want to focus on top-performing systems.
elements.sent-2411-144: Moreover, these correlations ignore whether and how LLM-based judgements impact the statistically significant differences among systems with respect to human assessments.
elements.sent-2411-145: In this work, we look at how LLM-generated judgements preserve ranking differences among top-performing systems and also how they preserve pairwise significance evaluation as human judgements.
elements.sent-2411-146: Our results show that LLM-based judgements are unfair at ranking top-performing systems.
elements.sent-2411-147: Moreover, we observe an exceedingly high rate of false positives regarding statistical differences.
elements.sent-2411-148: Our work represents a step forward in the evaluation of the reliability of using LLMs-based judgements for IR evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-149: We hope this will serve as a basis for other researchers to develop more reliable models for automatic relevance assessment.
elements.sent-2411-150: Test collections allow for easy evaluation and comparison of Information Retrieval (IR) systems.
elements.sent-2411-151: A test collection consists of a set of relevance judgements (also called labels, assessments or qrels throughout this paper), which are annotations establishing the documents systems should retrieve for each user's need (also known as topics).
elements.sent-2411-152: Collections built in venues like the Text REtrieval Conference (TREC) have enabled tremendous advancements in IR for many years.
elements.sent-2411-153: However, making the relevance judgements for a TREC-like test collection can be very expensive and labour-intensive.
elements.sent-2411-154: Low-budget collections could lead to biased and unstable evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-155: Recently, several works explored the possibility of using large language models (LLMs) to assess the relevance of documents~\cite{Upadhyay2024a,Upadhyay2024b,Abbasiantaeb2024,Rahmani2024a,Thomas2024,MacAvaney2023,Faggioli2023 , since they are considerable cheaper than humans and show strong text-understanding capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-156: All these works use ranking correlations, such as Kendall's $\tau$~\cite{Kendall1948 , to validate the reliability of judgements generated with an LLM for IR evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-157: The rationale is that if judgements generated by an LLM can provide a similar ranking of systems to the ranking inferred from human judgements, the LLM could replace the human in terms of capturing run-level effectiveness~\cite{Upadhyay2024b .}
elements.sent-2411-158: Thus, the size of future collections in terms of judgements could increase drastically since the budget would no longer be an issue.
elements.sent-2411-159: The problem is that Kendall's $\tau$ is not very informative for evaluating agreement on the top-performing systems.
elements.sent-2411-160: Usually, the goal of developing a new system is surpassing the top of the leaderboard.
elements.sent-2411-161: Therefore, it is vital to distinguish meaningful differences between the best systems.
elements.sent-2411-162: Furthermore, some recent works have demonstrated that rank correlation is not enough~\cite{Otero2023b,Kutlu2018b .}
elements.sent-2411-163: Although judgements generated by an LLM may give good results in terms of system rankings, we know nothing about whether the results of a statistical test between any two systems would yield similar results In this paper, we aim to contribute to fill this gap.
elements.sent-2411-164: First, we have a closer look at how LLM-generated qrels are able to preserve the same ranking of systems as inferred from human judgements regarding the top-performing systems.
elements.sent-2411-165: In particular, we use top-heavy rank correlations that penalize more those misses at the top.
elements.sent-2411-166: Second, we evaluate how fair LLM-generated qrels are on a per-run basis.
elements.sent-2411-167: Last, we evaluate if significance decisions regarding the differences between evaluated systems under LLM judgements are similar to those observed under human judgements.
elements.sent-2411-168: To this aim, we perform a statistical test between every pair of runs, both with human judgements and with LLM judgements, and see how both outputs agree.
elements.sent-2411-169: The main highlights of our results are the following.
elements.sent-2411-170: Correlations are not as good as they seem when we focus on the top-performing systems and degrade as we focus more and more on the first few systems.
elements.sent-2411-171: Additionally, there are many runs along the ranking that drop many positions when evaluated with LLM-based qrels, which suggests that they are not entirely fair.
elements.sent-2411-172: Finally, we observe that statistical tests made with LLM-based judgements yield an exceedingly high rate of false positives.
elements.sent-2411-173: Overall, our experiments suggest that LLMs are not yet ready to replace humans for making gold relevance assessments in IR offline evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-174: In this section, we have a closer look at how LLM-based qrels are able to preserve the same ranking of systems among top-performing ones.
elements.sent-2411-175: Each year, track organizers created a set of queries from which some received human judgements.
elements.sent-2411-176: It is important to note that the set of topics that have human judgements and the set of topics that have LLM judgements are disjoint every year.
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Statistics of the datasets used for experimentation. Note that $T_{H} \cap T_{LLM} = \emptyset$.} \label{tab:data} \setlength\tabcolsep{2pt} \ra{1.1} \begin{tabular}{@{}lcrrrrr@{}} Data DL-19 DL-20 DL-21 DL-22 DL-23 Topics with human judgements ($\lvert T_{H} \rvert$) 43 54 53 76 82 Topics with LLM judgements ($\lvert T_{LLM} \rvert$) 157 146 424 424 618 \arrayrulecolor{gray!30} \arrayrulecolor{black} Average judgements per topic in $T_{H}$ 215 211 204 5084 272 Average judgements per topic in $T_{LLM}$ 70 128 157 133 73 \arrayrulecolor{gray!30} \arrayrulecolor{black} Submissions 36 59 63 100 35 Pairs of submissions 630 1711 1953 4950 595 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-177: To evaluate to which extent LLM-based qrels can preserve the ordering among top-performing systems, we have computed two different top-weighted correlations, namely $\tau_{AP}$~\cite{Yilmaz2008 and Rank-Biased Overlap (RBO)~\cite{Webber2010 .}}
elements.sent-2411-178: The main idea is that, when comparing two rankings of systems, discrepancies among those systems with high positions are penalized more than those with low positions.
elements.sent-2411-179: As already demonstrated in previous work~\cite{Rahmani2024b,Upadhyay2024b,Faggioli2023 , LLM-based qrels show competitive values regarding Kendall's $\tau$.}
elements.sent-2411-180: However, we can observe that the correlations that penalize more for failures in higher positions give significantly lower values, which highlights the idea that the judgements generated with an LLM are not able to identify the best systems.
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{Systems Ranking correlations between official TREC human-made judgements and LLM-based judgements. $\tau_{AP}$ and RBO are top-weighted correlations.} \label{tab:correlation} \setlength\tabcolsep{2pt} \ra{1.1} \begin{tabular}{@{}lllrrrrr@{}} \multirow{2.3}{*}{Metric} \multicolumn{5}{c}{Dataset} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2019} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2020} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2021} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2022} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2023} \multirow{3}{*}{AP} $\tau$ 0.62 0.85 0.80 0.75 0.79 $\tau_{AP}$ 0.49 0.70 0.72 0.63 0.66 $RBO~(p = 0.7)$ 0.11 0.43 0.52 0.51 0.33 \multirow{3}{*}{NDCG} $\tau$ 0.80 0.90 0.87 0.85 0.88 $\tau_{AP}$ 0.79 0.84 0.85 0.77 0.83 RBO ($p$ = 0.7) 0.93 0.54 0.97 0.58 0.69 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-181: These correlations are useful for showing overall performance on large-scale experiments, but they hide the fact that some runs may be treated differently in the two evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-182: That is, it could be the case that, although correlations are actually high, some runs are losing (or gaining) many positions in the ranking.
elements.sent-2411-183: We cannot see this only with ranking correlations.
elements.sent-2411-184: Following a common approach in past work~\cite{Voorhees2018,Otero2023a , we evaluate the fairness.}
elements.sent-2411-185: When evaluated with LLM-generated judgements, we compute the change that each run suffers with respect to the ranking of systems inferred from human judgements.
elements.sent-2411-186: We shoe the results of this experiment in \Cref{fig:fairness .}
elements.sent-2411-187: This figure depicts the distributions, as boxplots, of the changes in ranking positions that runs undergo when evaluated with LLM-based assessments.
elements.sent-2411-188: A positive value means that the run is at a higher position with LLM qrels, while a negative value means that the run is at a lower position.
elements.sent-2411-189: Overall, we observe that almost all runs suffer some change in position when evaluated with LLM judgments.
elements.sent-2411-190: More worrying is the fact that there are, in all cases, regardless of the metric or the collection, runs that suffer changes of 5 or more positions, even reaching changes of more than 40 positions.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{plots/fairness-drops} \caption{Results of the fairness experiment. For each year and metric, we plot the distribution of the changes in ranking positions that runs undergo when evaluated with LLM-generated assessments. A positive change means that the run is ranked at a higher position under LLM judgements, while a negative value means that the run is ranked at a lower position.} \label{fig:fairness}
elements.sent-2411-191: As a follow-up analysis, in \Cref{fig:drop-runs we include the change suffered by each of the runs that participated in the DL-2020.}
elements.sent-2411-192: The x-axis shows the ranking of the runs inferred from human judgements, while the y-axis shows the change that each run suffers, in terms of ranking position, when evaluated with LLM judgements.
elements.sent-2411-193: Overall, we observe that most of the runs suffer a change.
elements.sent-2411-194: Only 10 and 13 runs stay at the same position when evaluated with AP and NDCG, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-195: In other words, 83\% of runs with AP, and 78\% with NDCG, get a different position when evaluated with LLM-based qrels.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{plots/drops-runs} \caption{Per-run drops in DL-2020. The x-axis shows the position of each run in the ranking inferred from human judgements. The y-axis shows the change in ranking position when evaluated with LLM-based judgements. A positive change means that the run is ranked at a higher position under LLM judgements, while a negative value means that the run is ranked at a lower position.} \label{fig:drop-runs}
elements.sent-2411-196: In the previous section, we evaluated biases exhibited by LLM-based relevance assessments in ranking correlations and ranking swaps, especially among top-performing systems.
elements.sent-2411-197: In this section, we study the reliability of LLM-generated judgements to evaluate retrieval systems from the perspective of statistical tests.
elements.sent-2411-198: Assuming that human judgements are the ground truth, we evaluate whether the LLM labels can provide a similar pairwise significance evaluation, i.e., whether decisions regarding the statistical difference between a pair of systems are the same using human labels and LLM labels.
elements.sent-2411-199: Using an evaluation measure of choice (e.g. Average Precision), we compute per-topic scores for each run in $R$ using both sets of judgements independently.
elements.sent-2411-200: Thus, we have two arrays of per-topic scores for each run, one obtained using $Q_{H}$ and the other obtained with $Q_{LLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-201: With these per-topic scores, we compute a $p$-value for each pair of runs~\footnote{We use a two-sided test.}
elements.sent-2411-202: So, in the end, we have, on the one hand, a $p$-value for each pair of runs computed from the scores obtained with $Q_{H}$ and, on the other hand, a different $p$-value for each pair of runs computed from the scores obtained with $Q_{LLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-203: To compute the $p$-values, we employed a two-sided Wilcoxon Signed-Rank test as recommended in previous literature~\cite{Parapar2021,Otero2025 , and set $\alpha = 0.05$.}
elements.figure-2411-010: \caption{Classification results observed using the synthetic qrels on different datasets with AP and NDCG.} \label{tab:agreements} \setlength\tabcolsep{2pt} \ra{1.1} \begin{tabular}{@{}lllrrrrr@{}} \multirow{2.3}{*}{Metric} \multicolumn{5}{c}{Dataset} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2019} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2020} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2021} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2022} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2023} \multirow{4.3}{*}{AP} TP 80\% 94\% 96\% 95\% 97\% FN 20\% 6\% 4\% 5\% 3\% \arrayrulecolor{gray!30} \arrayrulecolor{black} TN 29\% 42\% 17\% 20\% 11\% FP 71\% 58\% 83\% 80\% 89\% \multirow{4.3}{*}{NDCG} TP 92\% 98\% 98\% 97\% 98\% FN 8\% 2\% 2\% 3\% 2\% \arrayrulecolor{gray!30} \arrayrulecolor{black} TN 33\% 46\% 28\% 26\% 6\% FP 67\% 54\% 72\% 74\% 94\% \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-204: From this table, we highlight several interesting results.
elements.sent-2411-205: First, we see systematically better figures with NDCG than MAP---higher positives, lower negatives---, suggesting that NDCG is more robust for evaluating systems with LLM-qrels.
elements.sent-2411-206: We observe that LLM-generated labels detect most of the existing significant differences, since TP ratios are near 100\% almost in every case.
elements.sent-2411-207: In other words, some pairs of systems are not significantly different under $Q_{H}$ but they are under $Q_{LLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-208: It is a concerning result, since judgements created with an LLM would lead a researcher to incorrectly conclude and present a supposedly significant result.
elements.sent-2411-209: We performed this sampling 200 times, and computed the average of ratios over these iterations.
elements.figure-2411-011: \caption{Classification results observed using the SynDL synthetic qrels on different datasets with AP and NDCG. We randomly undersample $T_{LLM}$ topics such that $\lvert T_{LLM} \rvert = \lvert T_{H} \rvert$.} \label{tab:agreements-sampling} \setlength\tabcolsep{2pt} \ra{1.1} \begin{tabular}{@{}lllrrrrr@{}} \multirow{2.3}{*}{Metric} \multicolumn{5}{c}{Dataset} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2019} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2020} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2021} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2022} \multicolumn{1}{c}{DL-2023} \multirow{4.3}{*}{AP} TP 68\% 88\% 88\% 89\% 91\% FN 32\% 12\% 12\% 11\% 9\% \arrayrulecolor{gray!30} \arrayrulecolor{black} TN 57\% 62\% 50\% 51\% 28\% FP 43\% 38\% 50\% 49\% 72\% \multirow{4.3}{*}{NDCG} TP 83\% 94\% 91\% 92\% 93\% FN 17\% 6\% 9\% 8\% 7\% \arrayrulecolor{gray!30} \arrayrulecolor{black} TN 58\% 63\% 64\% 55\% 33\% FP 42\% 37\% 36\% 45\% 67\% \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-210: These results show that, indeed, under equal sample size conditions, the number of false positives is lower, supporting our previous hypothesis, although they are still concerning.
elements.sent-2411-211: We also observe lower values of true positives.
elements.sent-2411-212: In this case, we also observe better figures with NDCG, a result in line with previous research in related fields~\cite{Valcarce2018c .}
elements.sent-2411-213: Overall, the results of this experiment showcase that LLMs are not yet capable of producing IR relevance labels that preserve the same statistical differences as human labels.
elements.sent-2411-214: Similar to the previous section, we are also interested in whether a run has more or less significant differences when evaluated under the LLM-based qrels.
elements.sent-2411-215: For every run, we count the significant differences observed under human and LLM judgements against this run.
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{plots/significance-drops} \caption{Distribution of the drops in significance counts of the runs when evaluated with LLM labels. Each value is the average over 50 iterations of undersampling $T_{LLM}$.} \label{fig:drops}
elements.sent-2411-216: This figure shows the distribution of the per-run significance drops, averaged over 50 undersampling iterations, for the different year and effectiveness measures we have used.
elements.sent-2411-217: The ideal case here would be observing narrow boxes near 0 without outliers.
elements.sent-2411-218: However, we observe that, to a greater or lesser degree, there is always some run treated differently, regardless of the year and the effectiveness measure.
elements.sent-2411-219: This suggests that the false negatives we observed in a previous experiment (see \Cref{tab:agreements-sampling ) are concentrated within just a few runs.}
elements.sent-2411-220: In other words, the false negatives appear because the LLM qrels consistently fail to correctly evaluate the same few runs.
elements.sent-2411-221: Recent research has explored the possibility of using LLMs to assess the relevance of documents, showing promising results: LLMs can produce accurate labels compared to human assessments and reliable rankings of systems regarding overall ranking correlation.
elements.sent-2411-222: However, little work has evaluated the validity of these judgements in terms of distinguishing the best top-performing systems and preserving statistical significance stability.
elements.sent-2411-223: On the one hand, when developing and evaluating new IR systems, it is vital to distinguish meaningful differences between the best systems.
elements.sent-2411-224: On the other hand, the significant differences detected with human judgements and with LLM judgements might be completely different.
elements.sent-2411-225: In this work, we looked at these two issues and evaluated the reliability of LLM labels for IR evaluation from different perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-226: First, we evaluated if LLM-based qrels were able to correctly rank top-performing systems by employing top-heavy ranking correlation.
elements.sent-2411-227: Results showed that correlations degrade as we focus more and more on the best systems.
elements.sent-2411-228: We also evaluated whether significance decisions regarding the differences between evaluated systems are similar to those observed under human judgements.
elements.sent-2411-229: Our results showed that LLM-generated judgements yield an exceedingly high rate of false positives.
elements.sent-2411-230: This result suggests they are biased to incorrectly mark a significant difference when there is none.
elements.sent-2411-231: We also looked at whether any of the runs suffered significant changes in their significance when evaluated with LLM judgments.
elements.sent-2411-232: In this experiment, we observed that there are runs that suffer drops of up to 50 positions, which suggests that these LLM-generated judgements are not entirely fair for evaluating IR systems.
elements.sent-2411-233: Overall, our results showcase that LLMs are not yet ready to make gold relevance assessments for IR evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-234: We hope that this work serves as a basis for other research to build more reliable models for automatic relevance assessment in IR.

elements.sent-2411-236: The authors also thank the funding of project PID2022-137061OB-C21 (Ministerio de Ciencia e Innovación supported by the European Regional Development Fund).
elements.sent-2411-237: Recently, several works explored the possibility of using large language models (LLMs) to assess the relevance of documents~\cite{Upadhyay2024a,Upadhyay2024b,Upadhyay2024c,Abbasiantaeb2024,Rahmani2024a,Thomas2024,MacAvaney2023,Faggioli2023 , since they are considerable cheaper than humans and show strong text-understanding capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-238: In addition, recent studies have highlighted that LLM-based judgements may suffer from other issues~\cite{Alaofi2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-239: First, we have a closer look at how LLM-generated qrels are able to preserve the same ranking of systems as inferred from human judgements regarding top-performing systems.
elements.sent-2411-240: In particular, we use top-heavy rank correlations that penalize more misses at the top.
elements.sent-2411-241: For more details about how the LLM-based labels have been generated, we refer the reader to the paper~\cite{Rahmani2024b .}
elements.sent-2411-242: It is important to note that the set of topics that have human judgements and the set of topics that have LLM judgements are disjoint.
